suggestions.name: MapNumericConverter
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for converting map values to specific numeric types. They share common conversion logic through the getNumber method and follow identical patterns for type-safe numeric extraction. Extracting them creates a focused converter that separates numeric conversion concerns from general map utilities.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a single, well-defined responsibility: converting map values to specific numeric types. They all follow the same pattern - retrieve a value from a map by key, attempt to convert it to a Number (handling both Number objects and String representations), and then convert that Number to the requested primitive wrapper type (Byte, Double, Float, Integer, Long, Short). The getNumber method serves as the core conversion logic that all other methods depend on.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "type conversion" domain, specifically focused on numeric type conversion from map values. They represent a cohesive subsystem for safely extracting and converting numeric data from maps, handling null values gracefully and providing type-safe access to numeric map contents. This is a classic example of related conversion operations that work together.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates numeric conversion logic from general map utilities
- Code Reusability: The converter can be used independently by any class needing map-to-numeric conversions
- Complexity Reduction: Removes 7 related methods from the god class, making it more focused
- Testability: Numeric conversion logic can be tested in isolation with focused test cases
- Maintainability: Changes to numeric conversion behavior are centralized in one place

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate type conversion concerns. The methods exhibit high cohesion (all perform numeric conversions) and can operate with low coupling (only depend on standard Java types and NumberFormat). This extraction creates a specialized converter that adheres to the Single Responsibility Principle.

suggestions.name: MapValueExtractor
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for safely extracting typed values from maps with default value fallback. They share the same null-safe extraction pattern and delegate to a common `applyDefaultValue` method. Extracting them creates a focused class that handles the single responsibility of safe map value retrieval with type conversion and default value support.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a single, well-defined responsibility: safely extracting typed values from maps with default value fallback. They all follow the same pattern - take a map, a key, and a default value, then return either the converted map value or the default if the value is null/missing. The core logic is centralized in the `applyDefaultValue` method, which handles null safety and delegates to type-specific conversion functions.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "safe map value extraction" domain. They represent a cohesive abstraction for type-safe map access with default values. This is essentially a specialized form of map value conversion/extraction that handles edge cases (null maps, missing keys, conversion failures) gracefully by providing sensible defaults.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates safe map value extraction logic from other map utilities
- Code Reusability: This extractor can be used independently across different contexts that need safe map access
- Complexity Reduction: Removes 20+ related methods from the main MapUtils class, making it more focused
- Testability: Easier to test all edge cases for safe extraction in isolation
- Maintainability: Changes to default value handling logic are centralized

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate a cohesive set of related methods that handle a specific concern. The extracted class will have high cohesion (all methods perform safe value extraction) and low coupling (minimal dependencies, just needs the base conversion methods). This also follows the Single Responsibility Principle by creating a class focused solely on safe map value extraction with defaults.

suggestions.name: MapValueExtractor
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for safely extracting typed values from maps with fallback default functions. They share the same core algorithm and provide type-specific convenience methods, making them ideal candidates for extraction into a specialized accessor class that encapsulates safe map value retrieval logic.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share the primary responsibility of safely extracting typed values from maps with fallback behavior. They all follow the same pattern: attempt to retrieve and convert a value from a map using a key, and if that fails or returns null, invoke a default function to generate a fallback value. Each method handles a specific data type (Boolean, Byte, Double, Float, Integer, Long, Map, Number, Short, String) but uses the same underlying mechanism via applyDefaultFunction.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These methods represent the domain concept of "safe map value extraction with defaults". They form a cohesive subsystem for type-safe map access that gracefully handles missing keys or conversion failures. The shared applyDefaultFunction method serves as the core algorithm, while the typed methods provide convenient, type-specific entry points. This is essentially a specialized accessor pattern for maps with built-in fallback mechanisms.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates the concern of safe map value extraction from other map utilities
- Code reusability: The extracted class can be used independently wherever safe map access is needed
- Complexity reduction: Removes this specialized functionality from the broader MapUtils class, making both classes more focused
- Testability: Allows focused testing of the default function application logic and type conversion behavior in isolation

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate a cohesive set of related methods that implement the Strategy pattern (using Function interfaces for default value generation). The extraction creates a specialized accessor that encapsulates the complexity of safe map value retrieval with fallbacks, following the principle of high cohesion within the extracted class.

suggestions.name: MapDebugPrinter
suggestions.rationale: The debugging and printing methods form a cohesive unit responsible for visualizing Map structures with proper formatting, indentation, and cycle detection. Extracting these methods into a MapDebugPrinter class isolates the complex tree-traversal logic and improves the focus of the original MapUtils class.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a common responsibility around Map operations and debugging/printing functionality. The cluster includes utility methods for safe Map access (getObject, safeAddToMap, size), Map transformation (invertMap, toMap), and detailed Map visualization (debugPrint, verbosePrint, verbosePrintInternal, printIndent). The printing methods specifically handle complex nested Map structures with cycle detection and detailed formatting.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
The methods represent two distinct but related domains: basic Map operations and Map debugging/visualization. However, the most cohesive subset focuses on the debugging and printing functionality - these methods work together to provide detailed, formatted output of Map structures with indentation, cycle detection, and optional debug information. The verbosePrintInternal method is the core algorithm that the public methods delegate to.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Separates Map debugging/visualization from general utility operations
- Code reusability: The debugging functionality can be reused across different contexts beyond MapUtils
- Complexity reduction: Removes complex printing logic from the main utility class
- Testability: Debugging logic can be tested independently with different PrintStream implementations

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate concerns. The debugging methods form a cohesive unit with shared state (INDENT_STRING) and collaborate to provide a single service: detailed Map visualization. This extraction supports the Single Responsibility Principle by isolating the complex tree-traversal and formatting logic.
